prompt.name: setup
prompt.content:
Attention: The following instructions must be followed in order to successfully set up Storybook in this project. Do not skip steps or attempt to do them out of order.

Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by analyzing the codebase,
configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing example stories for 9 components.

Work through these steps in order. After each story file, run Vitest to verify it renders.
If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it passes before moving on.

### Step 1: Analyze the codebase

Before writing any stories, understand what the components need to render:

- Scan the project for context providers, theme systems, routers, stores, and i18n setups.
  Look at the app's entry point (e.g. `App.tsx`, `main.tsx`, `layout.tsx`) to see what
  providers wrap the component tree.
- Identify global CSS or style imports required for components to look correct.
- Note any path aliases configured in tsconfig or bundler config.
- Read `.storybook/main.ts` (or `main.js`) to find the `stories` glob patterns.
  Your story files must match those patterns to be picked up by Storybook.

### Step 2: Configure `.storybook/preview.ts` with decorators

Add decorators that wrap every story with the providers your components need.
Without this, most non-trivial components will crash.

If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` in `.storybook/preview.ts`):
```ts
// .storybook/preview.ts
import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles

import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview';

export const config = definePreview({
  decorators: [
    (Story) => (
      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
        <MemoryRouter>
          <Story />
        </MemoryRouter>
      </ThemeProvider>
    ),
  ],
});
```

Otherwise:
```ts
// .storybook/preview.ts
import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles

const preview = {
  decorators: [
    (Story) => (
      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
        <MemoryRouter>
          <Story />
        </MemoryRouter>
      </ThemeProvider>
    ),
  ],
};
export default preview;
```

Common decorators to add:
- **Theme providers** (e.g. ThemeProvider, MUI ThemeProvider, styled-components, Tailwind)
- **Router** (e.g. MemoryRouter, BrowserRouter mock)
- **State stores** (e.g. Redux Provider, Zustand, Jotai)
- **i18n** (e.g. IntlProvider, I18nextProvider)
- **Global CSS** — import global stylesheets at the top of preview.ts

### Step 3: Write stories for 9 components

Pick 9 real components from the codebase, 3 of each complexity level.
Use the title prefix `AI Generated/<Complexity>/<ComponentName>` so they are grouped
together in the Storybook sidebar.

**Simple (3 components)** — Presentational with few props, no internal state.
Examples: Button, Badge, Avatar, Icon, Label, Chip.
Title format: `AI Generated/Simple/<ComponentName>`

**Medium (3 components)** — Multiple visual variants or composed from simpler components.
Examples: Card, Alert, Input, Select, Tooltip, Tabs.
Title format: `AI Generated/Medium/<ComponentName>`

**Complex (3 components)** — Internal state, side effects, or deep composition.
Examples: Modal, DataTable, Form, Dropdown, Accordion, Sidebar.
Title format: `AI Generated/Complex/<ComponentName>`

For each component, create a `<ComponentName>.stories.ts` file next to the component.
Each file must have at least 2 story exports covering the component's main states.
Make sure the file location and naming matches the `stories` patterns in `.storybook/main.ts`.

If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` / `config.meta` patterns):

Story format (CSF Factory — this project uses CSF factories):
```ts
import { config } from '#.storybook/preview';
import { Button } from './Button';

const meta = config.meta({
  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
  component: Button,
});

export const Default = meta.story({
  args: {
    label: 'Click me',
  },
});

export const Disabled = meta.story({
  args: {
    label: 'Disabled',
    disabled: true,
  },
});
```

Otherwise:

Story format (CSF):
```ts
import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react';
import { Button } from './Button';

const meta = {
  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
  component: Button,
} satisfies Meta<typeof Button>;

export default meta;
type Story = StoryObj<typeof meta>;

export const Default: Story = {
  args: {
    label: 'Click me',
  },
};

export const Disabled: Story = {
  args: {
    label: 'Disabled',
    disabled: true,
  },
};
```

Rules:
- Every named export is a story. Use `args` to set props.
- Provide all required props via `args` — check the component's types.
- If a component needs per-story decorators (beyond the global ones), add them in the meta.
- Do NOT use `any` types. Use the component's prop types for type safety.

Reference: https://storybook.js.org/docs/latest/writing-stories

### Step 4: Verify each story with Vitest

After writing each story file, immediately verify it:

```bash
npx vitest --project storybook <path-to-story-file>
```

**Self-healing loop — repeat for every story file:**
1. Write/update the story file
2. Run `npx vitest --project storybook <path-to-story-file>`
3. If it fails: read the error output carefully
   - Missing provider → add a decorator in `.storybook/preview.ts` or in the story meta
   - Missing prop → add the required prop to `args`
   - Import error → fix the import path
   - CSS/asset error → add static dirs or import the stylesheet
4. Fix the issue and go back to step 2
5. Once the test passes, move to the next component

After all 9 story files pass individually, run the full suite:
```bash
npx vitest --project storybook
```

### Checklist

- [ ] Analyzed codebase for providers, global styles, and path aliases
- [ ] Read story patterns from `.storybook/main.ts`
- [ ] Configured `.storybook/preview.ts` with necessary decorators
- [ ] Simple component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Simple component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Simple component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Full Vitest suite passes: `npx vitest --project storybook`
- [ ] Run `npx storybook doctor` to check for common issues (version mismatches, duplicated deps, etc.)

### MEDIUM Components (Multiple variants or composed from simpler components)

#### 2. **ArticleList**
- **File Path:** `/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T19-25-05Z-f801cca5/project/frontend/src/components/ArticleList.tsx`

- **Event Handling:** Keyboard events (Enter/Space) and click handlers
- **Description:** Navigation component that renders a list of articles, each wrapped with Article component. Handles both click and keyboard interactions for accessibility.
- **Complexity Reasoning:** Composed of simpler Article components, has event handling and callbacks, but no internal state or hooks.

---

#### 3. **LanguageSelector**
- **File Path:** `/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T19-25-05Z-f801cca5/project/frontend/src/components/LanguageSelector.tsx`
- **Props:** None (standalone component)
- **Internal State:** 
  - `showDropdown: boolean` - controls dropdown visibility
- **Context Dependencies:** None directly, but uses `useLocalization()` hook
- **Hooks Used:** 
  - `useState()` - for dropdown state
  - `useEffect()` - for click-outside event listener
  - `useRef()` - for dropdown reference
- **External Dependencies:** 
  - `useLocalization` hook (manages language state and localStorage)
  - `LANGUAGES` constant from `languages.ts`
- **Description:** Dropdown component for language selection. Displays available languages with flags and handles click-outside detection to close dropdown.
- **Complexity Reasoning:** Has internal state and side effects (event listeners), but relatively straightforward logic. Single hook dependency that handles complex state. Could be Medium rather than Simple due to state and effects.

---

### COMPLEX Components (Internal state, side effects, deep composition, or context dependencies)

- **File Path:** `/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T19-25-05Z-f801cca5/project/frontend/src/components/WikiCard.tsx`
- **Props:**
  - `article: WikiArticle` (complex object with nested structure)
- **Internal State:**
  - `imageLoaded: boolean` - tracks image load status for lazy loading
- **Context Dependencies:** 
  - `useLikedArticles()` - requires `LikedArticlesProvider` context wrapper
- **Hooks Used:**
  - `useState()` - for image loading state
- **External Libraries:** `lucide-react` (for Share2 and Heart icons)
- **Features:**
  - Lazy image loading with opacity transitions
  - Image error handling with fallback
  - Like/unlike functionality (double-click and button)
  - Share functionality (native Web Share API with clipboard fallback)
  - Gradient overlays and backdrop blur effects
  - Animation for liked state
- **Provider Requirements:** Must be wrapped with `<LikedArticlesProvider>`
- **Description:** Full-screen card component displaying Wikipedia articles with rich features including image lazy-loading, likes system, sharing capabilities, and complex styling with gradients and animations.
- **Complexity Reasoning:** Multiple features, context dependency, side effects (image loading callbacks, error handling), integration with context for liked articles state, and complex visual layering with animations.

---

| WikiCard | Complex | 1 | Yes | Yes (useLikedArticles) | Yes (useState) |

---

- **LikedArticlesProvider** - Required wrapper for WikiCard component
  - Provides: `toggleLike()`, `isLiked()`, and `likedArticles` state
  - Also renders animated heart animation on like
  - File: `/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T19-25-05Z-f801cca5/project/frontend/src/contexts/LikedArticlesContext.tsx`

- **useLocalization Hook** - Required for LanguageSelector
  - Manages language state with localStorage persistence
  - File: `/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T19-25-05Z-f801cca5/project/frontend/src/hooks/useLocalization.ts`

---

## Frontend Project Analysis

**Framework:** React 18.3.1 with TypeScript 5.6.2
**Build Tool:** Vite 6.0.5
**Styling:** Tailwind CSS 4.0.3 (with @tailwindcss/vite)
**Package Manager:** npm (ES modules enabled via `"type": "module"`)

**Project Name:** WikiTok - A TikTok-style interface for exploring random Wikipedia articles

### 2. DIRECTORY STRUCTURE (src/)

```
src/
├── App.tsx                  # Main application component
├── main.tsx                 # Entry point
├── index.css                # Global styles (imports tailwindcss)
├── languages.ts             # Multi-language support config (20+ languages)
├── components/
│   ├── WikiCard.tsx         # Main card component displaying Wikipedia articles
│   ├── LanguageSelector.tsx # Language dropdown selector
│   ├── Article.tsx          # Generic article component
│   └── ArticleList.tsx      # Article list navigation component
├── contexts/
│   └── LikedArticlesContext.tsx  # Context for managing liked articles (localStorage-backed)
├── hooks/
│   ├── useWikiArticles.ts   # Fetches Wikipedia articles via API
│   └── useLocalization.ts   # Language preference management
├── assets/
│   ├── heartAnimation.css   # Heart animation styles
│   └── react.svg
├── styles/
│   └── Article.css          # Article component styles
├── types/
│   └── ArticleProps.ts      # Type definitions
└── stories/                 # Storybook example stories
    ├── Button.tsx/.stories.ts
    ├── Header.tsx/.stories.ts
    ├── Page.tsx/.stories.ts
    ├── Configure.mdx
    └── assets/              # Storybook example assets
```

**main.tsx** (/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T19-25-05Z-f801cca5/project/frontend/src/main.tsx):

- Contains About modal and Liked Articles modal

content.thinking:
OK now I have a complete picture. Let me plan my approach:

The App component uses `useWikiArticles` which makes real API calls to Wikipedia. This will be problematic for tests. I'll avoid using App as a story and instead focus on components that don't make network requests.

My 9 components will be:
1. **Simple - Button** (src/stories/Button.tsx)
2. **Simple - Article** (src/components/Article.tsx) 
3. **Simple - Header** (src/stories/Header.tsx) - no internal state, just renders based on props


4. **Medium - ArticleList** (src/components/ArticleList.tsx)
5. **Medium - LanguageSelector** (src/components/LanguageSelector.tsx)
6. **Medium - Page** (src/stories/Page.tsx) - has internal state for user login and composes Header
7. **Complex - WikiCard** (src/components/WikiCard.tsx) - needs the LikedArticlesProvider, manages state, and handles multiple user interactions
8. **Complex - App** (src/App.tsx) - very complex but I'll need to work around the API call issue
9. **Complex - LikedArticlesProvider** (src/contexts/LikedArticles) - context wrapper for managing liked articles across the app

For the App component, the useWikiArticles hook makes API calls inside a useEffect, but it's wrapped in error handling that just logs failures and leaves the component in a safe state. Even if the network request fails, the component should still render with a loading spinner and empty articles, so the test should pass without needing to mock the fetch call.

The main concern is that `window.location.reload()` is called in an onClick handler, but since it's not triggered during initial render, it shouldn't cause test failures. The useLocalization hook accesses localStorage, which should work fine in browser-based tests.

For the LikedArticlesProvider story, I need to create a helper component that demonstrates the provider's functionality by using the useLikedArticles hook to show the like/heart toggle behavior.

Writing the provider story component... I'm realizing there's a potential issue with nested providers here. The story wraps `LikeDemo` in its own `LikedArticlesProvider`, but the preview decorator already has one globally, so `useLikedArticles` inside `LikeDemo` will use the nearest parent provider from the story rather than the outer one, which means the global decorator becomes redundant in this case.

I could simplify by just rendering `LikeDemo` without the extra wrapper since the global provider already exists, but then the story wouldn't be demonstrating the provider itself. I think the better approach is to keep the provider in the story's render function so it actually showcases the `LikedArticlesProvider` component, and let the global decorator sit unused—that's fine.

Now I'm going to set up the preview configuration and write out all the stories.

Setting up preview.tsx... I'm checking how the different components handle imports—Article and Button use the classic React import pattern, while WikiCard relies on the automatic JSX transform. Since the project uses Vite with the modern JSX plugin, I don't need to import React in the preview file either.

Now I need to review the existing story files to make sure I don't create conflicts when writing the new ones.

transcript.prompt:
Attention: The following instructions must be followed in order to successfully set up Storybook in this project. Do not skip steps or attempt to do them out of order.

Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by analyzing the codebase,
configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing example stories for 9 components.

Work through these steps in order. After each story file, run Vitest to verify it renders.
If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it passes before moving on.

### Step 1: Analyze the codebase

Before writing any stories, understand what the components need to render:

- Scan the project for context providers, theme systems, routers, stores, and i18n setups.
  Look at the app's entry point (e.g. `App.tsx`, `main.tsx`, `layout.tsx`) to see what
  providers wrap the component tree.
- Identify global CSS or style imports required for components to look correct.
- Note any path aliases configured in tsconfig or bundler config.
- Read `.storybook/main.ts` (or `main.js`) to find the `stories` glob patterns.
  Your story files must match those patterns to be picked up by Storybook.

### Step 2: Configure `.storybook/preview.ts` with decorators

Add decorators that wrap every story with the providers your components need.
Without this, most non-trivial components will crash.

If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` in `.storybook/preview.ts`):
```ts
// .storybook/preview.ts
import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles

import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview';

export const config = definePreview({
  decorators: [
    (Story) => (
      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
        <MemoryRouter>
          <Story />
        </MemoryRouter>
      </ThemeProvider>
    ),
  ],
});
```

Otherwise:
```ts
// .storybook/preview.ts
import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles

const preview = {
  decorators: [
    (Story) => (
      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
        <MemoryRouter>
          <Story />
        </MemoryRouter>
      </ThemeProvider>
    ),
  ],
};
export default preview;
```

Common decorators to add:
- **Theme providers** (e.g. ThemeProvider, MUI ThemeProvider, styled-components, Tailwind)
- **Router** (e.g. MemoryRouter, BrowserRouter mock)
- **State stores** (e.g. Redux Provider, Zustand, Jotai)
- **i18n** (e.g. IntlProvider, I18nextProvider)
- **Global CSS** — import global stylesheets at the top of preview.ts

### Step 3: Write stories for 9 components

Pick 9 real components from the codebase, 3 of each complexity level.
Use the title prefix `AI Generated/<Complexity>/<ComponentName>` so they are grouped
together in the Storybook sidebar.

**Simple (3 components)** — Presentational with few props, no internal state.
Examples: Button, Badge, Avatar, Icon, Label, Chip.
Title format: `AI Generated/Simple/<ComponentName>`

**Medium (3 components)** — Multiple visual variants or composed from simpler components.
Examples: Card, Alert, Input, Select, Tooltip, Tabs.
Title format: `AI Generated/Medium/<ComponentName>`

**Complex (3 components)** — Internal state, side effects, or deep composition.
Examples: Modal, DataTable, Form, Dropdown, Accordion, Sidebar.
Title format: `AI Generated/Complex/<ComponentName>`

For each component, create a `<ComponentName>.stories.ts` file next to the component.
Each file must have at least 2 story exports covering the component's main states.
Make sure the file location and naming matches the `stories` patterns in `.storybook/main.ts`.

If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` / `config.meta` patterns):

Story format (CSF Factory — this project uses CSF factories):
```ts
import { config } from '#.storybook/preview';
import { Button } from './Button';

const meta = config.meta({
  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
  component: Button,
});

export const Default = meta.story({
  args: {
    label: 'Click me',
  },
});

export const Disabled = meta.story({
  args: {
    label: 'Disabled',
    disabled: true,
  },
});
```

Otherwise:

Story format (CSF):
```ts
import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react';
import { Button } from './Button';

const meta = {
  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
  component: Button,
} satisfies Meta<typeof Button>;

export default meta;
type Story = StoryObj<typeof meta>;

export const Default: Story = {
  args: {
    label: 'Click me',
  },
};

export const Disabled: Story = {
  args: {
    label: 'Disabled',
    disabled: true,
  },
};
```

Rules:
- Every named export is a story. Use `args` to set props.
- Provide all required props via `args` — check the component's types.
- If a component needs per-story decorators (beyond the global ones), add them in the meta.
- Do NOT use `any` types. Use the component's prop types for type safety.

Reference: https://storybook.js.org/docs/latest/writing-stories

### Step 4: Verify each story with Vitest

After writing each story file, immediately verify it:

```bash
npx vitest --project storybook <path-to-story-file>
```

**Self-healing loop — repeat for every story file:**
1. Write/update the story file
2. Run `npx vitest --project storybook <path-to-story-file>`
3. If it fails: read the error output carefully
   - Missing provider → add a decorator in `.storybook/preview.ts` or in the story meta
   - Missing prop → add the required prop to `args`
   - Import error → fix the import path
   - CSS/asset error → add static dirs or import the stylesheet
4. Fix the issue and go back to step 2
5. Once the test passes, move to the next component

After all 9 story files pass individually, run the full suite:
```bash
npx vitest --project storybook
```

### Checklist

- [ ] Analyzed codebase for providers, global styles, and path aliases
- [ ] Read story patterns from `.storybook/main.ts`
- [ ] Configured `.storybook/preview.ts` with necessary decorators
- [ ] Simple component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Simple component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Simple component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Full Vitest suite passes: `npx vitest --project storybook`
- [ ] Run `npx storybook doctor` to check for common issues (version mismatches, duplicated deps, etc.)

**App.tsx** (/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T19-25-05Z-f801cca5/project/frontend/src/App.tsx):
- Main application component
- Manages state: showAbout, showLikes, searchQuery
- Implements infinite scroll with IntersectionObserver
- Contains About modal and Liked Articles modal
- Integrates Vercel Analytics
- Uses custom hooks: useWikiArticles, useLikedArticles

### 4. CONTEXT PROVIDERS & THEME SYSTEMS

**LikedArticlesContext** (/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T19-25-05Z-f801cca5/project/frontend/src/contexts/LikedArticlesContext.tsx):
- Context for managing liked articles globally
- Stores liked articles in localStorage
- Provides methods: `toggleLike()`, `isLiked()`
- Shows heart animation on new like
- No theme system present; uses Tailwind CSS classes directly

**Localization:** Not a provider pattern but hooks-based via `useLocalization()`
- Stores language preference in localStorage
- Supports 20+ languages with Wikipedia API variants

### 5. GLOBAL CSS & STYLE IMPORTS

**index.css** (/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T19-25-05Z-f801cca5/project/frontend/src/index.css):
```css
@import "tailwindcss";
html, body { overscroll-behavior-y: contain; overflow: hidden; }
.hide-scroll { /* Hides scrollbars */ }
```

**Component-specific styles:**
- heartAnimation.css - CSS keyframe animations
- Article.css - Accessibility-focused article container styles
- Various button.css, header.css, page.css in stories/

**No global theme system** - All styling via Tailwind utility classes

### 6. PATH ALIASES

**No path aliases configured** in tsconfig.json or vite.config.ts. All imports use relative paths (e.g., `../contexts/LikedArticlesContext`)

### 7. STORYBOOK CONFIGURATION

```typescript
stories: [
  '../src/**/*.mdx',
  '../src/**/*.stories.@(js|jsx|mjs|ts|tsx)',
  './eval-support/*.mdx',
]
```

**Story glob patterns:**
- Any `.mdx` files in src/
- Stories named `*.stories.[js|jsx|mjs|ts|tsx]`
- Eval support MDX files in .storybook/eval-support/

**Framework:** @storybook/react-vite (pre-release version 0.0.0-pr-34421-sha-0f73e3a9)

**Addons:**
- @chromatic-com/storybook
- @storybook/addon-vitest
- @storybook/addon-a11y
- @storybook/addon-docs
- @storybook/addon-onboarding

### 8. STORYBOOK PREVIEW CONFIG

**preview.tsx** (/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-02T19-25-05Z-f801cca5/project/frontend/.storybook/preview.tsx):

**Note:** Does NOT use CSF Factory with `definePreview` - uses standard Preview type export. No global decorators configured.

**Production:**

- tailwindcss (4.0.3) with @tailwindcss/postcss and @tailwindcss/vite

**Data Fetching:**
- Direct Wikipedia API calls in useWikiArticles hook
